hero.name: Kazys
hero.subtitle: Building secure systems, automation tools and cybersecurity solutions that solve real problems.

items.label: Projects built across security and software
Technologies used in production and study

about.statement: I'm a Software Engineering student focused on cybersecurity. I build security tools, automation, and backend systems, and I'm drawn to offensive and defensive security and low-level internals.

items.title: Password Manager
items.description: A zero-knowledge password manager where all encryption happens client-side, so the server never sees plaintext credentials. Built on AES-256-GCM with Argon2id key derivation and non-extractable Web Crypto keys to resist XSS.

items.title: Kazz Injector
items.description: A Windows DLL injector built to study process internals. It implements both remote-thread LoadLibrary and manual PE mapping, with a live process list rendered in a hardware-accelerated ImGui interface.

items.title: PhishGuard
items.description: A real-time phishing detection platform that runs six analysis engines at once: homograph, domain age, SSL, brand impersonation, form analysis, and VirusTotal. A FastAPI backend powers a Chrome extension that scores URLs as you browse.

items.title: Network Radar
items.description: A real-time local-network monitor that discovers devices via ARP scanning and plots them on an animated radar, with vendor identification, port scanning, and live updates over WebSocket.

items.title: SolarHub
items.description: A premium, high-conversion landing page with layered animations, masked typography, real-time FAQ search, and full accessibility support including focus trapping and reduced-motion.

items.name: Offensive Security
items.description: Attacker mindset, exploit research, and offensive tooling.

items.name: Defensive Security
items.description: Detection, hardening, and phishing / threat analysis.

items.name: Backend Development
items.description: Secure APIs and services with Python, FastAPI and Node.js.

items.name: Reverse Engineering
items.description: Windows internals, binary analysis, and low-level systems.

items.name: Automation & Tooling
items.description: Scripts and tools that remove repetitive, manual work.

items.name: Network Analysis
items.description: Traffic inspection, scanning, and network diagnostics.

approach.heading: Behind every secure system is a deliberate engineering decision
approach.body: Security isn't a feature you bolt on at the end. It's a series of choices made while reading specs, modeling threats, and writing code. I build with that mindset from the first line.
gallery: Macro shot of a circuit board and network controller chip
Source code open in a dark editor
Hand sketching a system architecture map on a whiteboard
A developer workstation with dual monitors in a dim room

items.title: Research & Threat Modeling
items.description: Understand the system, its assets, and how it could realistically be attacked.

items.title: Design & Architecture
items.description: Plan a secure, maintainable structure before writing production code.

items.title: Implementation
items.description: Build with clean, defensive code and the right tools for the job.

items.title: Testing & Hardening
items.description: Validate, try to break it, and harden until it holds up under pressure.

education.title: Education & Learning

degree.description: Studying software engineering with a self-directed focus on cybersecurity, systems, and secure development.

education.tracksNote: Areas I'm actively studying and building in.

items.question: What technologies do you work with?
items.answer: Mainly Python, TypeScript, C++ and C#, with FastAPI and Node.js on the backend, React on the front-end, and security tooling like Wireshark, Nmap, Scapy and Burp Suite.

items.question: What areas of security interest you most?
items.answer: Both offensive and defensive security: exploit research and offensive tooling, plus detection, hardening, reverse engineering, and network analysis.

items.question: Are you open to collaboration?
items.answer: Yes. I'm interested in cybersecurity projects, backend systems, tool building, and research collaboration, whether solo or as part of a team.

items.question: Do you contribute to open source?
items.answer: I build and share security and automation tools, and I'm actively growing my open-source footprint. Feel free to reach out with ideas.

items.question: How can we work together?
items.answer: Send a message through the contact form or reach me on GitHub, LinkedIn, Email, Discord, or WhatsApp. Whatever's easiest for you.
